title: Filling and painting a textured Artex ceiling
subtitle: Flatten, fill, and paint a textured ceiling without full removal
excerpt: How to deal with a textured Artex ceiling without full removal: checking for asbestos, flattening the texture with texture paint or overlay compound, skimming for a smooth finish, and painting with the right roller.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. HSE guidance on artex and asbestos in domestic properties. British Gypsum White Book.

glossaryTerms.definition: A brand name for a water-based textured coating applied to ceilings and walls as a decorative finish. The name has become generic for any textured ceiling coating. Applied between the 1960s and 1980s, Artex may contain chrysotile asbestos fibres; this requires testing before any abrasive work.

A skim-thickness plastering compound applied over textured ceilings to flatten the surface. Applied with a trowel in thin layers; each layer fills the texture progressively without the mess of full removal.

content.text: ceilings were standard in UK housing from the 1960s to the 1990s. Removing them involves considerable mess and, in older properties, the risk of asbestos. The alternative is to work over the texture rather than through it: fill the peaks, skim a thin
over the whole surface, and paint the result. This takes a day rather than a weekend and produces a smooth painted ceiling without demolition.

If the ceiling was applied before 1985, treat it as potentially containing asbestos. Do not sand, abrade, or scrape Artex applied before 1985. Use a test kit (available from DIY merchants) or have a sample tested by a licensed laboratory. If asbestos is confirmed, the overlay-compound method described here is the safe approach; do not attempt removal yourself.

A plaster-based overlay compound such as Gyproc Easy Fill. A 150 mm taping knife and plasterer's trowel for larger areas. A pole sander with 120-grit mesh. A 9-inch deep-pile roller for the final coats. Cotton dust sheets and eye protection while working overhead.
Step 1, apply the first overlay coat

Mix the overlay compound to a smooth, pliable consistency. Apply with the taping knife in broad strokes across the ceiling, pressing the compound into the texture peaks. The aim of the first coat is to fill the deepest texture; do not try to achieve a flat surface on the first pass. Allow to dry completely (4 to 6 hours depending on room temperature) before the next coat.
Step 2, sand and apply a second coat

Sand the first coat lightly with the pole sander once fully dry to knock back any ridges. Do not sand aggressively; the goal is to level the surface, not to reach the old texture beneath. Apply the second coat in the same manner, working at right angles to the first coat's strokes. After the second coat dries and is sanded, the surface should be close to flat. A third coat may be needed in deep-textured areas.

Apply a diluted mist coat (emulsion diluted with water at 1:4) to seal the compound surface. Once dry, apply two full-strength ceiling emulsion coats with the deep-pile roller. A thick pile sleeve is worth using even on a nominally flat surface; the compound face is not as smooth as new plasterboard and a medium pile gives better coverage.

attrs.symptom: Overlay compound cracking as it dries
attrs.cause: Coat applied too thick in one pass.
attrs.fix: Multiple thin coats rather than one thick application. Sand between coats.

attrs.symptom: Texture still visible through paint after two coats
attrs.cause: Overlay coat insufficient to fully fill texture; or paint applied with a short-pile roller.
attrs.fix: Apply a further overlay coat, sand flat, and repaint with a deep-pile sleeve.
